mnemonics.title: Input Devices Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: MKD-TS-JL: 'Mice Keep Drawing - Touch Screens Join Laptops' - Stands for: Mouse, Keyboard, Digitizer, Touch Screen, Scanner, Joystick, Light Pen, Trackball, Tablet

mnemonics.title: Output Devices Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: C-P-M-L: 'Color Print Monitors Light' - Stands for: Cathode Ray Tube (CRT), Printer, Monitor (LCD/LED), Plotter

mnemonics.title: Pointing Devices
mnemonics.content: MOTH: 'Mouse Operates Through Hand' - Helps remember Mouse, Optical Mouse, Trackball, Handheld (light pen)

acronyms.usage: Old monitor technology using electron beams to light phosphorescent screen
Flat panel display using liquid crystals and backlight
Modern display tech with energy-efficient backlighting
Printer resolution measurement - higher DPI = better print quality
Standard connection port for modern input/output devices

visualTips.visualization: Picture your computer as a conversation: Input = Your mouth (speaking into the computer), Output = Your ears (hearing from the computer). Arrow pointing IN = Input, Arrow pointing OUT = Output
Imagine a magic drawing board: When you draw with the stylus, the tablet translates physical pen movements into digital coordinates on screen - like tracing on paper but creating computer files
Picture a flashlight pointing at a TV: The pen detects screen phosphors and sends coordinates to the computer - like touching exactly what you see on screen
Think of it as a glass window that can 'feel' your finger - it has invisible grid lines that detect where you touch, like pressing buttons on a smartphone
Mosaic tiles (raster = pixels) vs connect-the-dots (vector = lines/curves)

analogies.analogy: Like a hand moving a cursor on screen - just as your hand moves a pointer on paper, the mouse moves the cursor on computer
Like a photocopy machine but instead of paper copy, it creates a digital photo - as if taking a picture with a camera but of flat documents
Like a robotic artist that takes digital painting and puts it on paper - the computer tells it where to put ink dots, like an art teacher guiding a student
Like a mechanical pencil held by a robot arm - it draws precise lines on paper, used for architectural/engineering blueprints
Like a television specifically for computers - it displays the 'conversation' between you and the computer in visual form
Like a digital easel - artists can draw naturally with stylus just like on paper, but work appears directly on computer screen
